(**WA**) is the westernmost state of Australia. It is bounded by the Indian Ocean to the north and west, the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean") to the south, the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") to the north-east, and [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia") to the south-east. Western Australia is Australia's largest state, with a land area of 2,527,013 square kilometres (975,685 sq mi),[[5]](#cite_note-ga20-5) and is also the [second-largest subdivision](/wiki/List_of_first-level_administrative_divisions_by_area "List of first-level administrative divisions by area") of any country on Earth.[[a]](#cite_note-6)

first_paragraph: **Western Australia** (**WA**) is the westernmost state of Australia. It is bounded by the Indian Ocean to the north and west, the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean") to the south, the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") to the north-east, and [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia") to the south-east. Western Australia is Australia's largest state, with a land area of 2,527,013 square kilometres (975,685 sq mi),[[5]](#cite_note-ga20-5) and is also the [second-largest subdivision](/wiki/List_of_first-level_administrative_divisions_by_area "List of first-level administrative divisions by area") of any country on Earth.[[a]](#cite_note-6)

**Western Australia** (**WA**) is the westernmost state of Australia. It is bounded by the Indian Ocean to the north and west, the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean") to the south, the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") to the north-east, and [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia") to the south-east. Western Australia is Australia's largest state, with a land area of 2,527,013 square kilometres (975,685 sq mi),[[5]](#cite_note-ga20-5) and is also the [second-largest subdivision](/wiki/List_of_first-level_administrative_divisions_by_area "List of first-level administrative divisions by area") of any country on Earth.[[a]](#cite_note-6)

Western Australia has a diverse range of climates, including tropical conditions in the [Kimberley](/wiki/Kimberley_(Western_Australia) "Kimberley (Western Australia)"), deserts in the interior (including the [Great Sandy Desert](/wiki/Great_Sandy_Desert "Great Sandy Desert"), [Little Sandy Desert](/wiki/Little_Sandy_Desert "Little Sandy Desert"), [Gibson Desert](/wiki/Gibson_Desert "Gibson Desert"), and [Great Victoria Desert](/wiki/Great_Victoria_Desert "Great Victoria Desert")) and a [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") on the south-west and southern coastal areas. As of June 2024,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit) the state has 2.965 million inhabitants—10.9 percent of the national total.[[6]](#cite_note-7) Over 90 percent of the state's population live in the [south-west corner](/wiki/South-West_Land_Division "South-West Land Division") and around 80 percent live in the state capital [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth"),[[7]](#cite_note-ABSCapitalPop-8) leaving the remainder of the state sparsely populated. The [Trans-Australian Railway](/wiki/Trans-Australian_Railway "Trans-Australian Railway") and the [Eyre Highway](/wiki/Eyre_Highway "Eyre Highway") traverse the [Nullarbor Plain](/wiki/Nullarbor_Plain "Nullarbor Plain") in the state's south-east, providing the principal connection between Western Australia and the population centres in the [eastern states](/wiki/Eastern_states_of_Australia "Eastern states of Australia").

Western Australia's [Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Aboriginal_cultures_of_Western_Australia "Aboriginal cultures of Western Australia") have been present for tens of thousands of years. Dutch explorers visited Western Australia from the 17th century, with [Dirk Hartog](/wiki/Dirk_Hartog "Dirk Hartog")'s 1616 expedition the first Europeans to make landfall. The British claimed Western Australia in 1827 and established the [Swan River Colony](/wiki/Swan_River_Colony "Swan River Colony") with Perth as its capital in 1829. The [Western Australian gold rushes](/wiki/Western_Australian_gold_rushes "Western Australian gold rushes") of the late 19th century resulted in a significant population influx. The colony was granted [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") in 1890, the last of the Australian colonies to become self-governing, and [federated](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") with the other colonies in 1901.

Western Australia's [mining sector](/wiki/Mining_in_Western_Australia "Mining in Western Australia") is a key driver of the [state economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Western_Australia "Economy of Western Australia"). The late 20th century saw the development of the state's significant [iron ore mining industry](/wiki/Iron_ore_mining_in_Western_Australia "Iron ore mining in Western Australia") – the world's largest – as well as primarily offshore [petroleum and natural gas resources](/wiki/Petroleum_industry_in_Western_Australia "Petroleum industry in Western Australia"). [Gold mining](/wiki/Gold_mining_in_Western_Australia "Gold mining in Western Australia") retains a significant presence and many other mineral commodities are mined, with Perth being a major centre for associated services. Outside of mining, primary industry is the other significant contributor to the state's economy, including agriculture in the [Wheatbelt](/wiki/Wheatbelt_(Western_Australia) "Wheatbelt (Western Australia)") and temperate southern coastal regions, [pastoralism](/wiki/List_of_pastoral_leases_in_Western_Australia "List of pastoral leases in Western Australia") in marginal grassland areas, [forestry](/wiki/Forests_of_Australia "Forests of Australia") in the south-west and fishing (including [pearling](/wiki/Pearling_in_Western_Australia "Pearling in Western Australia") and, historically, [whaling](/wiki/Whaling_in_Western_Australia "Whaling in Western Australia")).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Western Australia](/wiki/History_of_Western_Australia "History of Western Australia")

See also: [French Western Australia](/wiki/French_Western_Australia "French Western Australia")

Western Australia has a geological history dating back to 4.4 billion years ago when [Hadean zircons](/wiki/Hadean_zircon "Hadean zircon") were formed. The earliest direct known life on land, known as [stromatolites](/wiki/Stromatolite "Stromatolite"), started to be created by microbes at around 3.48 billion years ago.

The first human inhabitants of Australia arrived from the north "over 50,000 years ago and possibly as much as 70,000 years ago".[[8]](#cite_note-men22-9): 5 Over thousands of years they eventually spread across the whole landmass.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") were long established throughout Western Australia by the time European explorers began to arrive in the early 17th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Hessel_Gerritsz_-_Malay_Archipelago_and_Australia.jpg/250px-Hessel_Gerritsz_-_Malay_Archipelago_and_Australia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hessel_Gerritsz_-_Malay_Archipelago_and_Australia.jpg)

Map first drawn in 1618 by [Hessel Gerritsz](/wiki/Hessel_Gerritsz "Hessel Gerritsz"), 12 years after [Europeans first encountered](/wiki/Janszoon_voyage_of_1605%E2%80%931606 "Janszoon voyage of 1605–1606") the Australian mainland,[[9]](#cite_note-huy95-10) showing the charted coast of Australia. Chartings after 1618, for example by [François Thijssen](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Thijssen "François Thijssen") in 1627, were added to the engraved plate between 1628 and 1632.[[10]](#cite_note-ger18-11)

The first Europeans to visit Western Australia were those of the Dutch [Dirk Hartog](/wiki/Dirk_Hartog "Dirk Hartog") expedition, who on 25 October 1616 landed at what is now known as Cape Inscription, [Dirk Hartog Island](/wiki/Dirk_Hartog_Island "Dirk Hartog Island"). For the rest of the 17th century, [other Dutch and British navigators](/wiki/European_maritime_exploration_of_Australia "European maritime exploration of Australia") encountered the coast of what [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman") named [New Holland](/wiki/New_Holland_(Australia) "New Holland (Australia)") in 1644, usually unintentionally as demonstrated by the many shipwrecks along the coast of ships that deviated from the [Brouwer Route](/wiki/Brouwer_Route "Brouwer Route") (because of poor navigation and storms).[[11]](#cite_note-12) By the late 18th century, British and French sailors had begun to explore the Western Australian coast. The [Baudin expedition](/wiki/Baudin_expedition_to_Australia "Baudin expedition to Australia") of 1800–03 included the coast of Western Australia and resulted in the [Freycinet Map of 1811](/wiki/Freycinet_Map_of_1811 "Freycinet Map of 1811"), the first published map featuring the full outline of Australia. The name New Holland remained in popular and semi-official use until at least the mid-1850s; that is, it was in use for about 206 years in comparison to the name Australia which to date has been in use for about 197 years.[[12]](#cite_note-wayb79-13): 11

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Thevenot_-_Hollandia_Nova_detecta_1644.png/250px-Thevenot_-_Hollandia_Nova_detecta_1644.png)](/wiki/File:Thevenot_-_Hollandia_Nova_detecta_1644.png)

[Melchisédech Thévenot](/wiki/Melchis%C3%A9dech_Th%C3%A9venot "Melchisédech Thévenot")'s *Hollandia Nova—Terre Australe* in his travel anthology (French: *Relations de divers voyages curieux*) published in 1664,[[13]](#cite_note-the64-14) and which seems to be a copy of *Archipelagus Orientalis sive Asiaticus* by [Joan Blaeu](/wiki/Joan_Blaeu "Joan Blaeu") in the *Atlas of the Great Elector* (German: *Atlas des Großen Kurfürsten*) from 1659.[[14]](#cite_note-bla59-15) The latitude staff depicted by Thévenot falls along the *Zaragoza antimeridian* from the [Treaty of Zaragoza](/wiki/Treaty_of_Zaragoza "Treaty of Zaragoza") of 1529 between [Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile") and [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), and which complemented the *Tordesillas meridian* from the [Treaty of Tordesillas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tordesillas "Treaty of Tordesillas") of 1494.

The origins of the present state began with the establishment by [Edmund Lockyer](/wiki/Edmund_Lockyer "Edmund Lockyer")[[15]](#cite_note-Western_Australia_Foundation-16) of a convict-supported settlement from [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") at [King George III Sound](/wiki/King_George_Sound "King George Sound"). The settlement was formally annexed on 21 January 1827 by Lockyer when he commanded the [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") be raised and a [feu de joie](/wiki/Feu_de_joie "Feu de joie") fired by the troops. The settlement was founded in response to British concerns about the possibility of a French colony being established on the coast of Western Australia.[[15]](#cite_note-Western_Australia_Foundation-16) On 7 March 1831 it was transferred to the control of the Swan River Colony,[[16]](#cite_note-knibbs-17) and named [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_Western_Australia "Albany, Western Australia") in 1832.

In 1829 the [Swan River Colony](/wiki/Swan_River_Colony "Swan River Colony") was established on the Swan River by Captain [James Stirling](/wiki/James_Stirling_(Australian_governor) "James Stirling (Australian governor)"). By 1832, the British settler population of the colony had reached around 1,500, and the official name of the colony was changed to Western Australia on 6 February that year.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19) The two separate townsites of the colony developed slowly into the port city of [Fremantle](/wiki/Fremantle "Fremantle") and the state's capital, [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth"). [York](/wiki/York,_Western_Australia "York, Western Australia") was the first inland settlement in Western Australia, situated 97 kilometres (60 mi) east of Perth and settled on 16 September 1831. York was the staging point for early explorers who discovered the rich gold reserves of Kalgoorlie.

Population growth was very slow until significant discoveries of gold were made in the 1890s around [Kalgoorlie](/wiki/Kalgoorlie "Kalgoorlie").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/John_Forrest_1898.jpg/250px-John_Forrest_1898.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Forrest_1898.jpg)

[John Forrest](/wiki/John_Forrest "John Forrest") was the first Premier of Western Australia.

In 1887, a new constitution was drafted, providing for the right of self-governance of European Australians, and in 1890, the act granting [self-government](/wiki/Self-governing_colony "Self-governing colony") to the colony was passed by the [British Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"). [John Forrest](/wiki/John_Forrest "John Forrest") became the first [Premier of Western Australia](/wiki/Premier_of_Western_Australia "Premier of Western Australia").

In 1896, after discoveries of gold at [Coolgardie](/wiki/Coolgardie,_Western_Australia "Coolgardie, Western Australia") and [Kalgoorlie](/wiki/Kalgoorlie "Kalgoorlie"), the [Western Australian Parliament](/wiki/Western_Australian_Parliament "Western Australian Parliament") authorised the raising of a loan to construct a [pipeline](/wiki/Pipeline_transport "Pipeline transport") to transport 23 megalitres (5 million imperial gallons) of water per day to the burgeoning population on the goldfields. The pipeline, known as the [Goldfields Water Supply Scheme](/wiki/Goldfields_Water_Supply_Scheme "Goldfields Water Supply Scheme"), was completed in 1903. [C. Y.  O'Connor](/wiki/C._Y._O%27Connor "C. Y. O'Connor"), Western Australia's first engineer-in-chief, designed and oversaw the construction of the pipeline. It carries water 530 km (330 mi) from Perth to [Kalgoorlie](/wiki/Kalgoorlie "Kalgoorlie"), and is attributed by historians as an important factor driving the state's population and economic growth.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

Following a campaign led by Forrest, electors of the colony of Western Australia voted in favour of [federation](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") with the five other Australian colonies, resulting in Western Australia officially becoming a state on 1 January 1901.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

### "West Australia"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: \"West Australia\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/2shillingsWestAustralia.jpg/250px-2shillingsWestAustralia.jpg)](/wiki/File:2shillingsWestAustralia.jpg)

"West Australia" on a 1902 stamp

"West Australia" and its related demonym "West Australian" are occasionally used, including in the names of the main daily newspaper, *[The West Australian](/wiki/The_West_Australian "The West Australian")*, and the state-based [West Australian Football League](/wiki/West_Australian_Football_League "West Australian Football League"), but are rarely used in an official sense. The terms "[Westralia](/wiki/Westralia_(disambiguation) "Westralia (disambiguation)")" and "Westralian" were regularly used in the 19th and 20th century.[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23) The terms are still found in the names of certain companies and buildings, e.g. Westralia House in Perth, the skyscraper Westralia Square on [St Georges Terrace](/wiki/St_Georges_Terrace "St Georges Terrace"), and Westralia Airports Corporation, which operates [Perth Airport](/wiki/Perth_Airport "Perth Airport"), as well as in the names of several ships.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Western Australia](/wiki/Geography_of_Western_Australia "Geography of Western Australia")

Western Australia is bounded to the east nominally by longitude 129°E, the meridian 129 degrees east of Greenwich, although the actual border with South Australia and the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") (as surveyed and marked or otherwise indicated on the ground) deviates from 129° east and is not a single straight line,[[25]](#cite_note-26) and bounded by the Indian Ocean to the west and north. The [International Hydrographic Organization](/wiki/International_Hydrographic_Organization "International Hydrographic Organization") (IHO) designates the [body of water south of the continent](/wiki/Australia_and_the_Southern_Ocean "Australia and the Southern Ocean") as part of the Indian Ocean; in Australia it is officially gazetted as the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean").[[b]](#cite_note-29)[[26]](#cite_note-iho53-27)[[27]](#cite_note-Canberra_all_at_sea-28)

The total length of the [state's eastern border](/wiki/Border_of_Western_Australia "Border of Western Australia") is 1,862 km (1,157 mi).[[28]](#cite_note-30) There are 20,781 km (12,913 mi) of coastline, including 7,892 km (4,904 mi) of island coastline.[[29]](#cite_note-31) The total land area occupied by the state is 2.5 million km2 (970 thousand mi2).[[30]](#cite_note-32)

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Geology")]

The bulk of Western Australia consists of the extremely old [Yilgarn craton](/wiki/Yilgarn_craton "Yilgarn craton") and [Pilbara craton](/wiki/Pilbara_craton "Pilbara craton") which merged with the [Deccan Plateau](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau") of India, [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar") and the [Kaapvaal](/wiki/Kaapvaal_craton "Kaapvaal craton") and [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") cratons of Southern Africa, in the [Archean](/wiki/Archean "Archean") Eon to form [Ur](/wiki/Ur_(continent) "Ur (continent)"), one of the oldest [supercontinents](/wiki/Supercontinent "Supercontinent") on Earth (3–3.2  billion years ago). In May 2017, evidence of the [earliest known life](/wiki/Earliest_known_life_forms "Earliest known life forms") [on land](/wiki/Evolutionary_history_of_life#Colonization_of_land "Evolutionary history of life") may have been found in 3.48-billion-year-old [geyserite](/wiki/Geyserite "Geyserite") and other related mineral deposits (often found around [hot springs](/wiki/Hot_spring "Hot spring") and [geysers](/wiki/Geyser "Geyser")) uncovered in the Pilbara craton.[[31]](#cite_note-PO-20170509-33)[[32]](#cite_note-NC-20170509-34)

Because the only [mountain-building](/wiki/Orogeny "Orogeny") since then has been of the [Stirling Range](/wiki/Stirling_Range "Stirling Range") with the rifting from [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), the land is extremely eroded and ancient, with no part of the state above 1,249 metres (4,098 ft) [AHD](/wiki/Australian_Height_Datum "Australian Height Datum") (at [Mount Meharry](/wiki/Mount_Meharry "Mount Meharry") in the [Hamersley Range](/wiki/Hamersley_Range "Hamersley Range") of the [Pilbara](/wiki/Pilbara "Pilbara") region). Most of the state is a low plateau with an average elevation of about 400 metres (1,200 ft), very low relief, and no [surface runoff](/wiki/Surface_runoff "Surface runoff"). This descends relatively sharply to the coastal plains, in some cases forming a sharp escarpment (as with the Darling Range/[Darling Scarp](/wiki/Darling_Scarp "Darling Scarp") near Perth).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/WAHighways.png/250px-WAHighways.png)](/wiki/File:WAHighways.png)

Western Australian cities, towns, settlements and [road network](/wiki/List_of_highways_in_Western_Australia "List of highways in Western Australia").

The extreme age of the landscape has meant that the soils are remarkably infertile and frequently [laterised](/wiki/Laterite "Laterite"). Even soils derived from [granitic](/wiki/Granite "Granite") [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock") contain an order of magnitude less available [phosphorus](/wiki/Phosphorus "Phosphorus") and only half as much [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") as soils in comparable climates in other continents. Soils derived from extensive sandplains or [ironstone](/wiki/Ironstone "Ironstone") are even less fertile, nearly devoid of soluble phosphate and deficient in [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), copper, [molybdenum](/wiki/Molybdenum "Molybdenum") and sometimes [potassium](/wiki/Potassium "Potassium") and [calcium](/wiki/Calcium "Calcium").

The infertility of most of the soils has required heavy application by farmers of fertilisers. These have resulted in damage to [invertebrate](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate") and bacterial populations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The grazing and use of hoofed mammals and, later, heavy machinery through the years have resulted in [compaction of soils](/wiki/Soil_compaction "Soil compaction") and great damage to the fragile soils.

Large-scale land clearing for agriculture has damaged habitats for native flora and fauna. As a result, the [South West region](/wiki/Southwest_Australia "Southwest Australia") of the state has a higher concentration of rare, threatened or endangered flora and fauna than many areas of Australia, making it one of the world's biodiversity "hot spots". Large areas of the state's wheatbelt region have problems with [dryland salinity](/wiki/Soil_salinity_control "Soil salinity control") and the loss of fresh water.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Western_Australia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Western_Australia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Western_Australia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") in Western Australia

The [southwest coastal area](/wiki/Southwest_corner_of_Western_Australia "Southwest corner of Western Australia") has a [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"). It was originally heavily forested, including large stands of [karri](/wiki/Karri "Karri"), one of the [tallest trees](/wiki/List_of_tallest_trees "List of tallest trees") in the world.[[33]](#cite_note-35) This agricultural region is one of the nine most bio-diverse terrestrial habitats, with a higher proportion of [endemic species](/wiki/Endemic_species "Endemic species") than most other equivalent regions. Thanks to the offshore [Leeuwin Current](/wiki/Leeuwin_Current "Leeuwin Current"), the area is one of the top six regions for marine biodiversity and contains the most southerly [coral reefs](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef") in the world.

Average annual rainfall varies from 300 millimetres (12 in) at the edge of the [Wheatbelt](/wiki/Wheatbelt_(Western_Australia) "Wheatbelt (Western Australia)") region to 1,400 millimetres (55 in) in the wettest areas near [Northcliffe](/wiki/Northcliffe,_Western_Australia "Northcliffe, Western Australia"), but from November to March, evaporation exceeds rainfall, and it is generally very dry. Plants are adapted to this as well as the extreme poverty of all soils.

The central two-thirds of the state is [arid](/wiki/Arid "Arid") and sparsely inhabited. The only significant economic activity is mining. Annual rainfall averages less than 300 millimetres (8–10 in), most of which occurs in sporadic torrential falls related to cyclone events in summer.[[34]](#cite_note-36)

An exception to this is the northern tropical regions. The [Kimberley](/wiki/Kimberley_(Western_Australia) "Kimberley (Western Australia)") has an extremely hot [monsoonal climate](/wiki/Australian_monsoon "Australian monsoon") with average annual rainfall ranging from 500 to 1,500 millimetres (20–60 in), but there is a very long almost rainless season from April to November. Eighty-five percent of the state's [runoff](/wiki/Runoff_(water) "Runoff (water)") occurs in the Kimberley, but because it occurs in violent floods and because of the insurmountable poverty of the generally shallow soils, the only development has taken place along the [Ord River](/wiki/Ord_River "Ord River").

Snow is rare in the state and typically occurs only in the [Stirling Range](/wiki/Stirling_Range "Stirling Range") near [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_Western_Australia "Albany, Western Australia"), as it is the only mountain range far enough south and sufficiently elevated. More rarely, snow can fall on the nearby [Porongurup Range](/wiki/Porongurup_National_Park "Porongurup National Park"). Snow outside these areas is a major event; it usually occurs in hilly areas of southwestern Australia. The most widespread low-level snow occurred on 26 June 1956 when snow was reported in the [Perth Hills](/wiki/Perth_Hills "Perth Hills"), as far north as [Wongan Hills](/wiki/Wongan_Hills,_Western_Australia "Wongan Hills, Western Australia") and as far east as [Salmon Gums](/wiki/Salmon_Gums,_Western_Australia "Salmon Gums, Western Australia"). However, even in the Stirling Range, snowfalls rarely exceed 5 cm (2 in) and rarely settle for more than one day.[[35]](#cite_note-37)

The highest observed temperature of 50.7 °C (123.3 °F) was recorded in [Onslow](/wiki/Onslow,_Western_Australia "Onslow, Western Australia") on 13 January 2022.[[36]](#cite_note-BBC-38) The lowest temperature recorded was −7.2 °C (19.0 °F) at [Eyre Bird Observatory](/wiki/Eyre_Bird_Observatory "Eyre Bird Observatory") on 17 August 2008.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

The [South Geomagnetic Pole](/wiki/South_Geomagnetic_Pole "South Geomagnetic Pole") is currently tilted 10 degrees away from the South Pole towards Western Australia, allowing for [aurora australis](/wiki/Aurora_australis "Aurora australis") displays as far north as [Geraldton](/wiki/Geraldton "Geraldton").[[38]](#cite_note-40)

### Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/RottnestQuokka.jpg/250px-RottnestQuokka.jpg)](/wiki/File:RottnestQuokka.jpg)

A [quokka](/wiki/Quokka "Quokka") on [Rottnest Island](/wiki/Rottnest_Island "Rottnest Island")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Black_Swans.jpg/250px-Black_Swans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Black_Swans.jpg)

The [black swan](/wiki/Black_swan "Black swan") is the state bird of Western Australia

Western Australia is home to around [630 species of birds](/wiki/List_of_Western_Australian_birds "List of Western Australian birds") (depending on the taxonomy used). Of these around 15 are [endemic](/wiki/Endemic_species "Endemic species") to the state. The best areas for birds are the southwestern corner of the state and the area around Broome and the Kimberley.

The [Flora of Western Australia](/wiki/Flora_of_Western_Australia "Flora of Western Australia") comprises 10,162 published native [vascular plant](/wiki/Vascular_plant "Vascular plant") species, along with a further 1,196 species currently recognised but unpublished. They occur within 1,543 [genera](/wiki/Genus "Genus") from 211 [families](/wiki/Family_(biology) "Family (biology)"); there are also 1,276 naturalised alien or [invasive plant](/wiki/Invasive_plant "Invasive plant") species, more commonly known as weeds.[[41]](#cite_note-43)[[42]](#cite_note-44) In the southwest region are some of the largest numbers of plant species for its area in the world.

Western Australia's [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") include the sandstone gorges of [the Kimberley](/wiki/Kimberley_tropical_savanna "Kimberley tropical savanna") on the northern coast, and below that the drier [Victoria Plains tropical savanna](/wiki/Victoria_Plains_tropical_savanna "Victoria Plains tropical savanna") inland, and the semi-desert [Pilbara shrublands](/wiki/Pilbara_shrublands "Pilbara shrublands"), [Carnarvon xeric shrublands](/wiki/Carnarvon_xeric_shrublands "Carnarvon xeric shrublands"), and [Western Australian mulga shrublands](/wiki/Western_Australian_mulga_shrublands "Western Australian mulga shrublands") to the southwest. Southwards along the coast are the [Southwest Australia savanna](/wiki/Southwest_Australia_savanna "Southwest Australia savanna") and the [Swan Coastal Plain](/wiki/Swan_Coastal_Plain "Swan Coastal Plain") around Perth, with the [Jarrah-Karri forest and shrublands](/wiki/Warren_bioregion "Warren bioregion") on the southwest corner of the coast around the [Margaret River](/wiki/Margaret_River_(wine) "Margaret River (wine)") wine-growing area. Going east along the Southern Ocean coast is the [Goldfields-Esperance](/wiki/Goldfields-Esperance "Goldfields-Esperance") region, including the [Esperance mallee](/wiki/Esperance_mallee "Esperance mallee") and the [Coolgardie woodlands](/wiki/Coolgardie_woodlands "Coolgardie woodlands") inland around town of [Coolgardie](/wiki/Coolgardie "Coolgardie"). Deserts occupy the interior, including the [Great Sandy-Tanami desert](/wiki/Great_Sandy-Tanami_desert "Great Sandy-Tanami desert"), [Gibson Desert](/wiki/Gibson_Desert "Gibson Desert"), [Great Victoria Desert](/wiki/Great_Victoria_Desert "Great Victoria Desert"), and [Nullarbor Plain](/wiki/Nullarbor_Plain "Nullarbor Plain").

In 1831, Scottish botanist [Robert Brown](/wiki/Robert_Brown_(botanist,_born_1773) "Robert Brown (botanist, born 1773)") produced a scientific paper, titled *[General view of the botany of the vicinity of Swan River](/wiki/General_view_of_the_botany_of_the_vicinity_of_Swan_River "General view of the botany of the vicinity of Swan River")*. It discusses the vegetation of the [Swan River Colony](/wiki/Swan_River_Colony "Swan River Colony").[[43]](#cite_note-45)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Demographics")]

See also: [Demographics of Australia](/wiki/Demographics_of_Australia "Demographics of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Perth_%28AU%29%2C_Elizabeth_Quay_--_2019_--_0259.jpg/250px-Perth_%28AU%29%2C_Elizabeth_Quay_--_2019_--_0259.jpg)](/wiki/File:Perth_(AU),_Elizabeth_Quay_--_2019_--_0259.jpg)

The metropolitan area of Western Australia's capital and largest city, [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth"), is home to more than 80% of the state's population.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Western_Australia_population_T.svg/250px-Western_Australia_population_T.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Western_Australia_population_T.svg)

Population growth 1829–2010

Europeans began to settle permanently in 1826 when [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_Western_Australia "Albany, Western Australia") was claimed by Britain to forestall French claims to the western third of the continent. Perth was founded as the [Swan River Colony](/wiki/Swan_River_Colony "Swan River Colony") in 1829 by British and Irish settlers, though the outpost languished. Its officials eventually requested [convict](/wiki/Convictism_in_Australia "Convictism in Australia") labour to augment its population. By the time [transportation](/wiki/Penal_transportation "Penal transportation") ceased in 1868, convicts outnumbered free settlers 9,700 to 7,300.[[44]](#cite_note-nma23-46) In the 1890s, interstate immigration, resulting from a mining boom in the [Goldfields region](/wiki/Goldfields-Esperance "Goldfields-Esperance"), resulted in a sharp population increase.

Western Australia did not receive significant flows of [immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_Australia "Immigration to Australia") from Britain, Ireland or elsewhere in the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") until the early 20th century. At that time, its local projects—such as the [Group Settlement Scheme](/wiki/Group_Settlement_Scheme "Group Settlement Scheme") of the 1920s, which encouraged farmers to settle the southwest—increased awareness of Australia's western third as a destination for colonists.

Led by immigrants from the British Isles, Western Australia's population developed at a faster rate during the twentieth century than it had previously. After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), both the eastern states and Western Australia received large numbers of [Italians](/wiki/Italian_Australian "Italian Australian"), [Croatians](/wiki/Croatian_Australian "Croatian Australian") and [Macedonians](/wiki/Macedonian_Australians "Macedonian Australians"). Despite this, Britain has contributed the greatest number of immigrants to this day. Western Australia—particularly Perth—has the highest proportion of British-born of any state: 10.3% in 2011, compared to a national average of 5.1%. This group is heavily concentrated in certain parts, where they account for a quarter of the population.[[45]](#cite_note-abs_2011-47)

Further information: [List of places in Western Australia by population](/wiki/List_of_places_in_Western_Australia_by_population "List of places in Western Australia by population")

Perth's metropolitan area (including [Mandurah](/wiki/Mandurah "Mandurah")) had an estimated population of 2,043,138[[7]](#cite_note-ABSCapitalPop-8) in June 2017 (79% of the state). Other significant population centres include [Bunbury](/wiki/Bunbury,_Western_Australia "Bunbury, Western Australia") (73,989),[[46]](#cite_note-ABSSUA-48) [Geraldton](/wiki/Geraldton "Geraldton") (37,961),[[46]](#cite_note-ABSSUA-48) [Kalgoorlie-Boulder](/wiki/Kalgoorlie-Boulder "Kalgoorlie-Boulder") (30,420),[[46]](#cite_note-ABSSUA-48) [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_Western_Australia "Albany, Western Australia") (33,998),[[46]](#cite_note-ABSSUA-48) [Karratha](/wiki/Karratha "Karratha") (16,446),[[46]](#cite_note-ABSSUA-48) [Broome](/wiki/Broome,_Western_Australia "Broome, Western Australia") (14,501)[[46]](#cite_note-ABSSUA-48) and [Port Hedland](/wiki/Port_Hedland,_Western_Australia "Port Hedland, Western Australia") (14,285).[[46]](#cite_note-ABSSUA-48)

According to the 2021 census, Christianity is the major religious affiliation in WA, followed by 41.1% of its population. ## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Economy of Western Australia](/wiki/Economy_of_Western_Australia "Economy of Western Australia") and [Economy of Australia](/wiki/Economy_of_Australia "Economy of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Brockman_4_Process_Plant%2C_July_2019_03.jpg/250px-Brockman_4_Process_Plant%2C_July_2019_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brockman_4_Process_Plant,_July_2019_03.jpg)

[Brockman 4](/wiki/Brockman_4_mine "Brockman 4 mine"), an [iron ore mine](/wiki/Iron_ore_mining_in_Western_Australia "Iron ore mining in Western Australia") in the [Pilbara](/wiki/Pilbara "Pilbara")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/2007_Resource_production_WA-svg.svg/250px-2007_Resource_production_WA-svg.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2007_Resource_production_WA-svg.svg)

Western Australia's resource commodity mix, 2007

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Major_West_Australian_Commodities_2008-2009_%28%24_million%29.png/250px-Major_West_Australian_Commodities_2008-2009_%28%24_million%29.png)](/wiki/File:Major_West_Australian_Commodities_2008-2009_($_million).png)

Major commodity mix, 2008–2009

Western Australia's economy is largely driven by extraction and processing of a diverse range of mineral and petroleum commodities. The structure of the economy is closely linked to these natural resources, providing a comparative advantage in resource extraction and processing. As a consequence:

* Western Australia contributes an estimated 58% of Australia's Mineral and Energy Exports,[[52]](#cite_note-58) potentially earning up to 4.64% of Australia's total GDP.[[53]](#cite_note-59)
* [Gross state product](/wiki/Gross_state_product "Gross state product") per person ($97,940 in 2017–18) is higher than any other state and well above the national average ($73,267).[[54]](#cite_note-60)
* Diversification (i.e. a greater *range* of commodities) over the past 15 years has provided a more balanced production base and less reliance on just a few major export markets, insulating the economy from fluctuations in world prices to some extent.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Finance, insurance and property services and construction have grown steadily and have increased their share of economic output.[[55]](#cite_note-61)
* Recent growth in global demand for minerals and petroleum, especially in China (iron-ore) and Japan (for LNG), has ensured economic growth above the national average.

In 2019 Western Australia's overseas exports accounted for 46% of the nation's total.[[56]](#cite_note-wsj-62)[[57]](#cite_note-63) The state's major export commodities included iron-ore, petroleum, gold, [alumina](/wiki/Alumina "Alumina"), nickel, wheat, copper, lithium, chemicals and mineral sands.[[58]](#cite_note-profile-64)

Western Australia is the world's largest iron-ore producer (34% of the world's total), and extracts 66% (6.9% of world production) of Australia's 306 tonnes (9.8 million troy ounces) of gold in 2022.[[59]](#cite_note-ga24-65) It is a major world producer of bauxite, which is processed into alumina at four refineries providing 11% of total world production. Until 2020 diamonds were extracted from the world's largest [diamond mine](/wiki/Argyle_diamond_mine "Argyle diamond mine") in the far north Kimberley region. Coal mined at [Collie](/wiki/Collie,_Western_Australia "Collie, Western Australia") is the main fuel for baseload electricity generation in the state's south-west.[[58]](#cite_note-profile-64)[[60]](#cite_note-usgs-66)

Agricultural production in WA is a major contributor to the state and national economy. In the period 2010–2019 wheat production in WA has averaged nearly 10 million tonnes (22 billion pounds), valued at $2.816 billion in 2019, accounting for half the nation's total and providing $2–3 billion in export income.[[61]](#cite_note-cropreport-67)[[62]](#cite_note-WAatAglance-68)

Other significant farm output includes wool, beef, lamb, barley, canola, lupins, oats and pulses.[[61]](#cite_note-cropreport-67) There is a high level of overseas demand for live animals from WA, driven mainly by southeast Asia's feedlots and Middle Eastern countries, where [Islamic dietary laws](/wiki/Islamic_dietary_laws "Islamic dietary laws") and a lack of storage and refrigeration facilities favour live animals over imports of processed meat. About half of Australia's live cattle exports come from Western Australia.[[63]](#cite_note-LivestockReport-69)

Resource sector growth in recent years has resulted in significant labour and skills shortages, leading to recent efforts by the state government to encourage interstate and overseas immigration.[[64]](#cite_note-70) According to the 2006 census,[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][[65]](#cite_note-71) the median individual income was A$500 per week in Western Australia (compared to A$466 in Australia as a whole). The median family income was A$1246 per week (compared to A$1171 for Australia). Recent growth has also contributed to significant rises in average property values in 2006, although values plateaued in 2007.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Whale_oil_tanks_at_Albany%27s_Historic_Whaling_Station%2C_April_2022_01.jpg/250px-Whale_oil_tanks_at_Albany%27s_Historic_Whaling_Station%2C_April_2022_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Whale_oil_tanks_at_Albany%27s_Historic_Whaling_Station,_April_2022_01.jpg)

[Albany's Historic Whaling Station](/wiki/Cheyne_Beach_Whaling_Station "Cheyne Beach Whaling Station")

Located south of Perth, the heavy industrial area of [Kwinana](/wiki/City_of_Kwinana "City of Kwinana") had the [nation's largest oil refinery](/wiki/Kwinana_Oil_Refinery "Kwinana Oil Refinery") with a capacity of 23,200 cubic metres per day (146,000 barrels per day) until its closure in 2021, producing most of the state's petrol and diesel.[[66]](#cite_note-72)[[67]](#cite_note-73)[[68]](#cite_note-74) Kwinana also hosts alumina and nickel processing plants, port facilities for grain and other bulk exports, and support industries for mining and petroleum such as heavy and light engineering, and metal fabrication. Shipbuilding (e.g. [Austal](/wiki/Austal "Austal")) and associated support industries are found at nearby [Henderson](/wiki/Henderson,_Western_Australia "Henderson, Western Australia"), just north of Kwinana. Significant secondary industries include cement and building product manufacturing, flour milling, food processing, animal feed production, automotive body building and printing.

Western Australia has a significant fishing industry. Products for local consumption and export include [western rock lobsters](/wiki/Western_rock_lobster "Western rock lobster"), prawns, crabs, shark and tuna, as well as pearl fishing in the [Kimberley](/wiki/Kimberley_(Western_Australia) "Kimberley (Western Australia)") region of the state. Processing is conducted along the west coast. [Whaling](/wiki/Whaling "Whaling") was a key marine industry but ceased at Albany in 1978.

Western Australia has the world's biggest plantations of both [Indian sandalwood](/wiki/Indian_sandalwood "Indian sandalwood") (northern WA)[[69]](#cite_note-75) and [Australian sandalwood](/wiki/Australian_sandalwood "Australian sandalwood") (semi-arid regions), which are used to produce [sandalwood oil](/wiki/Sandalwood_oil "Sandalwood oil") and [incense](/wiki/Incense "Incense").[[70]](#cite_note-waspfaq-76) The WA [sandalwood](/wiki/Sandalwood "Sandalwood") industry provides about 40% of the international sandalwood oil market.[[71]](#cite_note-abcdutjahn-77)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Broome_Cable_Beach_and_camels.jpg/250px-Broome_Cable_Beach_and_camels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Broome_Cable_Beach_and_camels.jpg)

Camel rides are a popular tourist activity at [Cable Beach](/wiki/Cable_Beach "Cable Beach") in [Broome](/wiki/Broome,_Western_Australia "Broome, Western Australia")

In the 2000s, tourism grew in importance, with significant numbers of visitors to the state in 2008 coming from the UK and Ireland (28%), other European countries (14%) Singapore (16%), Japan (10%) and Malaysia (8%).[[62]](#cite_note-WAatAglance-68) Revenue from tourism is a strong economic driver in many of the smaller population centres outside of Perth, especially in coastal locations.

Tourism forms a major part of the Western Australian economy with 833,100 international visitors making up 12.8% of the total international tourism to Australia in the year ending March 2015. The top three source markets include the United Kingdom (17%), Singapore (10%), and New Zealand (10%) with the majority of purpose for visitation being holiday/vacation reasons.[[72]](#cite_note-78) The tourism industry contributes $9.3 billion to the Western Australian economy and supports 94,000 jobs within the state. Both directly and indirectly, the industry makes up 3.2% of the state's economy whilst comparatively, WA's largest revenue source, the mining sector, brings in 31%.[[73]](#cite_note-79)

Tourism WA is the government agency responsible for promoting Western Australia as a holiday destination.[[74]](#cite_note-80)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Government")]

Main article: [Government of Western Australia](/wiki/Government_of_Western_Australia "Government of Western Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/AUS_Perth%2C_Central_Business_District%2C_Parliament_House_002.jpg/250px-AUS_Perth%2C_Central_Business_District%2C_Parliament_House_002.jpg)](/wiki/File:AUS_Perth,_Central_Business_District,_Parliament_House_002.jpg)

[Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Perth "Parliament House, Perth")

Western Australia was granted self-government in 1890[[75]](#cite_note-81) with a bicameral [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Western_Australia "Parliament of Western Australia") located in Perth, consisting of the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Western_Australian_Legislative_Assembly "Western Australian Legislative Assembly") (or *lower house*), which has 59 members; and the [Legislative Council](/wiki/Western_Australian_Legislative_Council "Western Australian Legislative Council") (or *upper house*), which has 36 members. Suffrage is universal and compulsory for citizens over 18 years of age.

With the federation of the Australian colonies in 1901, Western Australia became a state within Australia's [federal](/wiki/Federation "Federation") structure; this involved ceding certain powers to the Commonwealth (or Federal) government in accordance with the Constitution; all powers not specifically granted to the Commonwealth remained solely with the State. However over time the Commonwealth has effectively expanded its powers through broad interpretation of its enumerated powers and increasing control of taxation and financial distribution (see [Federalism in Australia](/wiki/Federalism_in_Australia "Federalism in Australia")).

Whilst the sovereign of Western Australia is [the monarch of Australia](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia") (currently [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")) and executive power is nominally vested in his or her state representative, the [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Western_Australia "Governor of Western Australia") (currently [Chris Dawson](/wiki/Chris_Dawson_(governor) "Chris Dawson (governor)")), executive power rests with the premier and ministers drawn from the party or coalition of parties holding a majority of seats in the Legislative Assembly. [Roger Cook](/wiki/Roger_Cook_(politician) "Roger Cook (politician)") is the premier, having succeeded [Mark McGowan](/wiki/Mark_McGowan "Mark McGowan") after his resignation in June 2023.

### Secession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Secession")]

Main article: [Secessionism in Western Australia](/wiki/Secessionism_in_Western_Australia "Secessionism in Western Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Westralia_secession_movement_meeting_1933.jpg/250px-Westralia_secession_movement_meeting_1933.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westralia_secession_movement_meeting_1933.jpg)

A 1933 meeting of the Dominion League in support of secession

Secessionism has been a recurring feature of Western Australia's political landscape since shortly after European settlement in 1826. Western Australia was the most reluctant participant in the [Commonwealth of Australia](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia").[[76]](#cite_note-82) Western Australia did not participate in the earliest federation conference. Longer-term residents of Western Australia were generally opposed to federation; however, the discovery of gold brought many immigrants from other parts of Australia. It was these residents, primarily in Kalgoorlie but also in Albany who voted to join the Commonwealth, and the proposal of these areas being admitted separately under the name [Auralia](/wiki/Auralia "Auralia") was considered.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In a [referendum in April 1933](/wiki/1933_Western_Australian_secession_referendum "1933 Western Australian secession referendum"), 68% of voters voted for the state to leave the Commonwealth of Australia with the aim of returning to the British Empire as an autonomous territory. The State Government sent a delegation to [Westminster](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), but the British Government ruled that after the [Statute of Westminster 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931"), it no longer had the authority to amend the constitution of Australia without the consent of its federal government; the British Government took no action.[[77]](#cite_note-83)

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Local government")]

Western Australia is divided into 139 [Local Government Areas](/wiki/Local_Government_Areas_of_Western_Australia "Local Government Areas of Western Australia"), including [Christmas Island](/wiki/Shire_of_Christmas_Island "Shire of Christmas Island") and the [Cocos (Keeling) Islands](/wiki/Shire_of_Cocos "Shire of Cocos"). Their mandate and operations are governed by the [Local Government Act 1995](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1995 "Local Government Act 1995").[[78]](#cite_note-84)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/UWAWinthropHallSunsetcurves_gobeirne.jpg/250px-UWAWinthropHallSunsetcurves_gobeirne.jpg)](/wiki/File:UWAWinthropHallSunsetcurves_gobeirne.jpg)

[University of Western Australia](/wiki/University_of_Western_Australia "University of Western Australia")

Education in Western Australia consists of one year of pre-school at age 4 or 5, followed by six years of primary education for all students as of 2015.[[79]](#cite_note-85) At age 12 or 13, students begin six years of secondary education. Students are required to attend school up until they are 16 years old. 16- and 17-year-olds are required to be enrolled in school or a training organisation, be employed or be in a combination of school/training/employment.[[80]](#cite_note-86) Students have the option to study at a [TAFE](/wiki/Technical_and_further_education "Technical and further education") college after Year 10,[[81]](#cite_note-87) or continue through to Year 12 with vocational courses or university entrance courses.[[82]](#cite_note-88)

There are five universities in Western Australia. They consist of four [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth")-based [public universities](/wiki/Public_university "Public university"), being the [University of Western Australia](/wiki/University_of_Western_Australia "University of Western Australia"), [Curtin University](/wiki/Curtin_University "Curtin University"), [Edith Cowan University](/wiki/Edith_Cowan_University "Edith Cowan University") and [Murdoch University](/wiki/Murdoch_University "Murdoch University"); and one [Fremantle](/wiki/Fremantle "Fremantle")-based [private Roman Catholic university](/wiki/Private_university "Private university"), the [University of Notre Dame Australia](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame_Australia "University of Notre Dame Australia"). The [University of Notre Dame](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame_Australia "University of Notre Dame Australia") is also one of only two [private universities](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") in Australia, along with [Bond University](/wiki/Bond_University "Bond University"), a not-for-profit private education provider based in [Gold Coast, Queensland](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland").

## Media

### Print

Main article: [List of newspapers in Western Australia](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_Western_Australia "List of newspapers in Western Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/OIC_herdsman_newspaper_house_3.jpg/250px-OIC_herdsman_newspaper_house_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:OIC_herdsman_newspaper_house_3.jpg)

[Seven West Media](/wiki/Seven_West_Media "Seven West Media")'s Newspaper House, where *[The West Australian](/wiki/The_West_Australian "The West Australian")* newspaper is produced

Western Australia has two daily newspapers: the [Seven West Media](/wiki/Seven_West_Media "Seven West Media")-owned tabloid *[The West Australian](/wiki/The_West_Australian "The West Australian")* and the *[Kalgoorlie Miner](/wiki/Kalgoorlie_Miner "Kalgoorlie Miner")*. Also published is one weekend paper, *The Weekend West*, and one Sunday tabloid newspaper, which is also owned by Seven West Media after purchase from [News Corporation's](/wiki/News_Corp_Australia "News Corp Australia") *[The Sunday Times](/wiki/The_Sunday_Times_(Western_Australia) "The Sunday Times (Western Australia)")*. There are also 17 weekly [community newspapers](/wiki/Community_paper "Community paper") with distribution from [Yanchep](/wiki/Yanchep "Yanchep") in the north to [Mandurah](/wiki/Mandurah "Mandurah") in the south. There are two major weekly rural papers in the state, *Countryman* and the [Australian Community Media](/wiki/Australian_Community_Media "Australian Community Media")-owned *[Farm Weekly](/wiki/Farm_Weekly "Farm Weekly")*. The national broadsheet publication *[The Australian](/wiki/The_Australian "The Australian")* is also available, although with sales per capita lagging far behind those in other states. *[WAtoday](/wiki/WAtoday "WAtoday")* is an online newspaper owned by [Nine Entertainment](/wiki/Nine_Entertainment "Nine Entertainment"), focusing its coverage on Perth and Western Australia.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Arts and entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Arts and entertainment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Hackett_Hall%2C_Western_Australian_Museum%2C_Perth%2C_2023%2C_01.jpg/250px-Hackett_Hall%2C_Western_Australian_Museum%2C_Perth%2C_2023%2C_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hackett_Hall,_Western_Australian_Museum,_Perth,_2023,_01.jpg)

[WA Museum Boola Bardip](/wiki/WA_Museum_Boola_Bardip "WA Museum Boola Bardip")

Western Australia is home to one of the country's leading performance training institutions, the acclaimed [Western Australian Academy of Performing Arts](/wiki/Western_Australian_Academy_of_Performing_Arts "Western Australian Academy of Performing Arts") (WAAPA), as well as a burgeoning theatrical and musical scene. Notable musicians and bands to have been born in or lived in Western Australia include [Adam Brand](/wiki/Adam_Brand_(musician) "Adam Brand (musician)"), [Ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia_(band) "Ammonia (band)"), [Karnivool](/wiki/Karnivool "Karnivool"), [Birds of Tokyo](/wiki/Birds_of_Tokyo "Birds of Tokyo"), [Bon Scott](/wiki/Bon_Scott "Bon Scott"), [Eskimo Joe](/wiki/Eskimo_Joe "Eskimo Joe"), [Johnny Young](/wiki/Johnny_Young "Johnny Young"), [Gyroscope](/wiki/Gyroscope_(band) "Gyroscope (band)"), the [John Butler Trio](/wiki/John_Butler_Trio "John Butler Trio"), [Tame Impala](/wiki/Tame_Impala "Tame Impala"), [Kevin Mitchell](/wiki/Kevin_Mitchell_(musician) "Kevin Mitchell (musician)"), [Tim Minchin](/wiki/Tim_Minchin "Tim Minchin"), [Troye Sivan](/wiki/Troye_Sivan "Troye Sivan"), [the Kill Devil Hills](/wiki/The_Kill_Devil_Hills "The Kill Devil Hills"), [Pendulum](/wiki/Pendulum_(drum_and_bass_band) "Pendulum (drum and bass band)"), [the Pigram Brothers](/wiki/The_Pigram_Brothers "The Pigram Brothers"), [Rolf Harris](/wiki/Rolf_Harris "Rolf Harris"), [Stella Donnelly](/wiki/Stella_Donnelly "Stella Donnelly") and [the Triffids](/wiki/The_Triffids "The Triffids"). The [West Australian Music Industry Awards](/wiki/West_Australian_Music_Industry_Awards "West Australian Music Industry Awards") (WAMis) have been awarded every year to the leading musicians and performers in WA since 2001.

Notable actors and television personalities from Western Australia include [Heath Ledger](/wiki/Heath_Ledger "Heath Ledger"), [Sam Worthington](/wiki/Sam_Worthington "Sam Worthington"), [Ernie Dingo](/wiki/Ernie_Dingo "Ernie Dingo"), [Jessica Marais](/wiki/Jessica_Marais "Jessica Marais"), [Megan Gale](/wiki/Megan_Gale "Megan Gale"), [Rove McManus](/wiki/Rove_McManus "Rove McManus"), [Isla Fisher](/wiki/Isla_Fisher "Isla Fisher"), and [Melissa George](/wiki/Melissa_George "Melissa George"). Films and television series filmed or partly filmed in Western Australia include *[Rabbit-Proof Fence](/wiki/Rabbit-Proof_Fence "Rabbit-Proof Fence")*, *[The Heights](/wiki/The_Heights_(Australian_TV_series) "The Heights (Australian TV series)")*, *[Mystery Road](/wiki/Mystery_Road_(TV_series) "Mystery Road (TV series)")*, *[These Final Hours](/wiki/These_Final_Hours "These Final Hours")*, *[Cloudstreet](/wiki/Cloudstreet "Cloudstreet")*, *[Jasper Jones](/wiki/Jasper_Jones_(film) "Jasper Jones (film)")*, *[Australia](/wiki/Australia_(2008_film) "Australia (2008 film)")*, *[Bran Nu Dae](/wiki/Bran_Nue_Dae_(film) "Bran Nue Dae (film)")*, *[Red Dog](/wiki/Red_Dog_(film) "Red Dog (film)")*, *[ABBA: the Movie](/wiki/ABBA:_the_Movie "ABBA: the Movie")* and *[Last Train to Freo](/wiki/Last_Train_to_Freo "Last Train to Freo")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Art_Gallery_of_WA.jpg/250px-Art_Gallery_of_WA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Art_Gallery_of_WA.jpg)

[Art Gallery of Western Australia](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_Western_Australia "Art Gallery of Western Australia")

Noted Western Australian Indigenous painters and artisans include [Jack Dale Mengenen](/wiki/Jack_Dale_Mengenen "Jack Dale Mengenen"), [Paddy Bedford](/wiki/Paddy_Bedford "Paddy Bedford"), [Queenie McKenzie](/wiki/Queenie_McKenzie "Queenie McKenzie"), and siblings [Nyuju Stumpy Brown](/wiki/Nyuju_Stumpy_Brown "Nyuju Stumpy Brown") and [Rover Thomas](/wiki/Rover_Thomas "Rover Thomas").[[83]](#cite_note-abc-89)

The [West Australian Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/West_Australian_Symphony_Orchestra "West Australian Symphony Orchestra") (WASO) is based at the [Perth Concert Hall](/wiki/Perth_Concert_Hall,_Western_Australia "Perth Concert Hall, Western Australia"). Other concert, performance and indoor sporting venues in Western Australia include [His Majesty's Theatre](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Theatre,_Western_Australia "His Majesty's Theatre, Western Australia"), the [State Theatre Centre of Western Australia](/wiki/State_Theatre_Centre_of_Western_Australia "State Theatre Centre of Western Australia"), the [Crown Theatre](/wiki/Crown_Perth "Crown Perth") and [Perth Arena](/wiki/Perth_Arena "Perth Arena"), which opened in 2012. Performing arts companies based in Perth include the [West Australian Ballet](/wiki/West_Australian_Ballet "West Australian Ballet"), the [West Australian Opera](/wiki/West_Australian_Opera "West Australian Opera"), the [Black Swan State Theatre Company](/wiki/Black_Swan_State_Theatre_Company "Black Swan State Theatre Company") and the [Perth Theatre Company](/wiki/Perth_Theatre_Company "Perth Theatre Company").

Western Australia has served as the setting for a number of works of [Australian literature](/wiki/Australian_literature "Australian literature"). Prominent authors include [Katharine Susannah Prichard](/wiki/Katharine_Susannah_Prichard "Katharine Susannah Prichard"), [Randolph Stow](/wiki/Randolph_Stow "Randolph Stow"), [Tim Winton](/wiki/Tim_Winton "Tim Winton"), [Kim Scott](/wiki/Kim_Scott "Kim Scott"), [Sally Morgan](/wiki/Sally_Morgan_(artist) "Sally Morgan (artist)"), [Joan London](/wiki/Joan_London_(Australian_author) "Joan London (Australian author)"), [Mary Durack](/wiki/Mary_Durack "Mary Durack") and [Craig Silvey](/wiki/Craig_Silvey "Craig Silvey").

The public [Art Gallery of Western Australia](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_Western_Australia "Art Gallery of Western Australia") is part of the [Perth Cultural Centre](/wiki/Perth_Cultural_Centre "Perth Cultural Centre"). Founded in 1895, it houses the State Art Collection, comprising works from local and international artists, dating back to the 1800s.[[84]](#cite_note-90) The [Perth Cultural Centre](/wiki/Perth_Cultural_Centre "Perth Cultural Centre") is also home to the [Western Australian Museum](/wiki/Western_Australian_Museum "Western Australian Museum"), [State Library of Western Australia](/wiki/State_Library_of_Western_Australia "State Library of Western Australia"), [State Records Office](/wiki/State_Records_Office_of_Western_Australia "State Records Office of Western Australia"), and [Perth Institute of Contemporary Arts](/wiki/Perth_Institute_of_Contemporary_Arts "Perth Institute of Contemporary Arts") (PICA). Western Australia has a [public library system](/wiki/Public_library "Public library"), with [libraries in every local government area](/wiki/List_of_libraries_in_Western_Australia#Public_libraries "List of libraries in Western Australia") (although some public libraries are shared school libraries).

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Sport")]

Main article: [Sport in Western Australia](/wiki/Sport_in_Western_Australia "Sport in Western Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Shaun_McManus_attempts_a_tackle_during_his_farewell_match.jpg/250px-Shaun_McManus_attempts_a_tackle_during_his_farewell_match.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shaun_McManus_attempts_a_tackle_during_his_farewell_match.jpg)

Matches between the two Western Australian teams, the [West Coast Eagles](/wiki/West_Coast_Eagles "West Coast Eagles") and the [Fremantle Dockers](/wiki/Fremantle_Dockers "Fremantle Dockers"), are known as the [Western Derby](/wiki/Western_Derby "Western Derby")

A number of national or international sporting teams and events are based in the state, including:

* [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football"): The [West Coast Eagles](/wiki/West_Coast_Eagles "West Coast Eagles") and the [Fremantle Dockers](/wiki/Fremantle_Dockers "Fremantle Dockers") compete in the [Australian Football League](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League") (AFL). They also have women's teams playing in the [AFL Women's](/wiki/AFL_Women%27s "AFL Women's") league. The [West Australian Football League](/wiki/West_Australian_Football_League "West Australian Football League") (WAFL) is the main local football competition, but other [local and amateur football leagues](/wiki/Australian_rules_football_in_Western_Australia "Australian rules football in Western Australia") exist across the state.
* Baseball: The [Perth Heat](/wiki/Perth_Heat "Perth Heat") compete in the [Australian Baseball League](/wiki/Australian_Baseball_League "Australian Baseball League").
* Basketball: The [Perth Wildcats](/wiki/Perth_Wildcats "Perth Wildcats") (men) and [Perth Lynx](/wiki/Perth_Lynx "Perth Lynx") (women) compete in the [National Basketball League](/wiki/National_Basketball_League_(Australasia) "National Basketball League (Australasia)") and [Women's National Basketball League](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_League "Women's National Basketball League"), respectively.
* [Cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"): [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia_cricket_team "Western Australia cricket team") represent the state in [first-class](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") and [List A](/wiki/List_A_cricket "List A cricket") domestic cricket, with the [Perth Scorchers](/wiki/Perth_Scorchers "Perth Scorchers") competing in the [Twenty20](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") [Big Bash League](/wiki/Big_Bash_League "Big Bash League").
* [Field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"): The [Thundersticks](/wiki/WA_Thundersticks "WA Thundersticks") (men) and Diamonds (women) compete in the [Australian Hockey League](/wiki/Australian_Hockey_League "Australian Hockey League").
* [Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"): The [Perth Thunder](/wiki/Perth_Thunder "Perth Thunder") (men) and [Perth Inferno](/wiki/Perth_Inferno "Perth Inferno") (women) compete in [Australian Ice Hockey League (AIHL).](/wiki/Australian_Ice_Hockey_League "Australian Ice Hockey League")
* [Netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball"): The [West Coast Fever](/wiki/West_Coast_Fever "West Coast Fever") compete in the [ANZ Championship](/wiki/ANZ_Championship "ANZ Championship").
* [Rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"): The [West Coast Pirates](/wiki/West_Coast_Pirates "West Coast Pirates") compete in the [S. G. Ball Cup](/wiki/S._G._Ball_Cup "S. G. Ball Cup").
* [Rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"): The [Western Force](/wiki/Western_Force "Western Force") competes in [Super Rugby Pacific](/wiki/Super_Rugby_Pacific "Super Rugby Pacific").
* [Soccer](/wiki/Soccer "Soccer"): [Perth Glory](/wiki/Perth_Glory "Perth Glory") field [men's](/wiki/Perth_Glory_FC "Perth Glory FC") and [women's](/wiki/Perth_Glory_FC_W-League "Perth Glory FC W-League") teams in the [A-League Men](/wiki/A-League_Men "A-League Men") and [A-League Women](/wiki/A-League_Women "A-League Women"), respectively.
* Tennis: The [ITF](/wiki/International_Tennis_Federation "International Tennis Federation") [Hopman Cup](/wiki/Hopman_Cup "Hopman Cup"), an annual international team indoor hardcourt tennis tournament.
* [Water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo"): The [UWA Torpedoes](/wiki/UWA_Torpedoes "UWA Torpedoes") water polo club competes in the [National Water Polo League (NWPL)](/wiki/Australian_National_Water_Polo_League "Australian National Water Polo League").
* [Formula 1](/wiki/Formula_1 "Formula 1"): The Formula 1 driver [Daniel Ricciardo](/wiki/Daniel_Ricciardo "Daniel Ricciardo") is from Perth.

International sporting events hosted in the past in Western Australia include the [Tom Hoad Cup](/wiki/Tom_Hoad_Cup "Tom Hoad Cup") (water polo), the [Perth International](/wiki/Perth_International "Perth International") (golf), the 2006 [Gravity Games](/wiki/Gravity_Games "Gravity Games") ([extreme sports](/wiki/Extreme_sports "Extreme sports")), the 2002 [Women's Hockey World Cup](/wiki/Women%27s_Hockey_World_Cup "Women's Hockey World Cup"), the 1991 [FINA World Aquatics Championships](/wiki/FINA_World_Aquatics_Championships "FINA World Aquatics Championships"), the [World Rally Championships](/wiki/World_Rally_Championships "World Rally Championships") and the [1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1962_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Aravina_Estate_%2823244523213%29.jpg/250px-Aravina_Estate_%2823244523213%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aravina_Estate_(23244523213).jpg)

The [Margaret River region](/wiki/Margaret_River_(wine_region) "Margaret River (wine region)") is known for its wine production

Western Australia's largest sports stadium is [Perth Stadium](/wiki/Perth_Stadium "Perth Stadium"), also known by naming rights sponsorship as Optus Stadium. It has a capacity of over 60,000 people and is primarily used for Australian rules football and cricket.

### Wine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Wine")]

Main article: [Western Australian wine](/wiki/Western_Australian_wine "Western Australian wine")

Winemaking regions are concentrated in the cooler climate of the [south-western portion](/wiki/West_Australian_wine#South_Western_Australia "West Australian wine") of the state. Western Australia produces less than 5% of the country's wine output, but in quality terms is considered to be very much near the top.[[85]](#cite_note-91)[[86]](#cite_note-Sotheby-92)[[87]](#cite_note-western_australian_wine-93)[[88]](#cite_note-ocw-wa-94) Major wine producing regions include: [Margaret River](/wiki/Margaret_River_(wine_region) "Margaret River (wine region)"), [The Great Southern](/wiki/Great_Southern_Wine_Region "Great Southern Wine Region"), [Swan Valley](/wiki/Swan_Valley_(Western_Australia) "Swan Valley (Western Australia)") as well as smaller districts including [Blackwood Valley](/wiki/Blackwood_Valley "Blackwood Valley"), [Manjimup](/wiki/Manjimup,_Western_Australia "Manjimup, Western Australia"), [Pemberton](/wiki/Pemberton,_Western_Australia "Pemberton, Western Australia"), [Peel](/wiki/Peel_(Western_Australia) "Peel (Western Australia)"), Chittering Valley, [Perth Hills](/wiki/Perth_Hills "Perth Hills"), and [Geographe](/wiki/Geographe_Bay "Geographe Bay").[[87]](#cite_note-western_australian_wine-93)

## Sister states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Australia&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sister states")]

Western Australia has five sister states:[[89]](#cite_note-95)

* [East Java](/wiki/East_Java "East Java"), Indonesia
* [Hyōgo Prefecture](/wiki/Hy%C5%8Dgo_Prefecture "Hyōgo Prefecture"), Japan
* [Andhra Pradesh State](/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh"), India
* [Tuscany Region](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany"), Italy
* [Zhejiang Province](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), China

In 1981, a [sister state](/wiki/Sister_city "Sister city") agreement was drawn up between Western Australia and [Hyōgo Prefecture](/wiki/Hy%C5%8Dgo_Prefecture "Hyōgo Prefecture") in Japan that was aimed at improving cultural ties between the two states.[[90]](#cite_note-96)[[91]](#cite_note-Hyogo-97) To commemorate the 10th anniversary of this agreement, the Hyōgo Prefectural Government Cultural Centre was established in Perth in 1992.[[92]](#cite_note-98) Prior to that, the Western Australian government opened an office in [Kobe](/wiki/Kobe "Kobe"), the largest city in Hyōgo, to facilitate maintenance of the relationship in 1989.[[91]](#cite_note-Hyogo-97)[[93]](#cite_note-garden-99)

Following the [Great Hanshin earthquake](/wiki/Great_Hanshin_earthquake "Great Hanshin earthquake") that devastated southern Hyōgo in January 1995, Western Australian groups and businesses raised funds and provided materials, whilst individuals travelled to Hyōgo to help with emergency relief and the subsequent reconstruction process.[[94]](#cite_note-100)[[95]](#cite_note-101)[[96]](#cite_note-102) The two governments signed a [memorandum of understanding](/wiki/Memorandum_of_understanding "Memorandum of understanding") on the 20th anniversary in 2001 that aimed to improve the economic relationship between the two states.[[91]](#cite_note-Hyogo-97)

Further to the sister state relationship, the [City of Rockingham](/wiki/City_of_Rockingham "City of Rockingham") in Western Australia and the [City of Akō](/wiki/Ak%C5%8D,_Hy%C5%8Dgo "Akō, Hyōgo") in Hyōgo signed a sister city agreement in 1997. It is one of nine sister city relationships between Western Australian and Japanese cities.[[97]](#cite_note-103)

Indigenous identification is separate to the ancestry question on the Australian Census and persons identifying as Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander may identify any ancestry.
